Lab Guide › Working in the Lab

Be safe

Lab safety culture, PPE, required trainings, and facility-specific requirements.

The safety of every member of our team and community is of the utmost importance. We take our safety responsibilities seriously and commit to creating and upholding a safe lab culture.

If you see anything that seems unsafe — or even slightly raises a question — ask and act immediately rather than waiting. Raise any concern directly to Christina. Suggestions that make the lab safer are always appreciated.

Member rights and obligations

You have the right to know about hazards in your lab space, obtain hazard-specific training, and work in a properly designed, equipped, and maintained facility.

You are responsible for participating in hazard-identification training, following UC Davis and BIRD Lab safety policies, correcting unsafe acts within your ability, and reporting any unsafe situation without fear of retaliation.

Minimum personal protective equipment (PPE)

  • Closed-toe shoes (no crocs).
  • Full-length pants.

Additional equipment may be required depending on what you’re using. No food or drink is allowed in the main wind tunnel room (Room 1402).

Required training (all members, before working in the office)

  1. Sign up for MAE annual safety training at the earliest date and get the certificate of completion.
  2. Complete the relevant LMS modules and keep the certificates:
    • Hazard Communication (everyone)
    • PPE: Personal Protective Equipment (everyone; covered if you’ve done UC Laboratory Safety Fundamentals)
    • Hand and Power Tool Safety (experimental work)
    • Ladder Safety (anyone using ladders)
    • Electrical Safety (wind-tunnel operators)
  3. Review all relevant Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) and Safety Data Sheets (SDS) for what you’ll use.
  4. Complete the “Initial Safety Training — Office and WT” workflow in Slack, including site-specific training with your assigned trainer.

Recurring: complete the annual refresher (each fall, during a lab meeting) and the MAE Annual Safety Orientation; send proof to the lab manager.

Facility-specific training

Wind tunnel operation. Review the wind-tunnel safety section of the Experimenters Safety Document and complete a walkthrough with the PI.

Working with live animals at CALI 🦅

Raptors are essentially flying knives. A high standard of safety in this facility is critical — for the birds and for people.

  1. Complete the ACU 101 course if you’ll work with live animals (re-take the ACU 101 refresher every 3 years).
  2. Read Care and Use of Birds, Owls and Raptors (includes zoonotic-disease review).
  3. Review Animal Bites and Scratches procedures and the reporting process.
  4. Review Animal Allergies information.
  5. Complete all California Raptor Center requirements (ask Christina for current guidelines).
  6. Complete the “Initial Safety Training — CALI” workflow in Slack, including the OHSS Risk Assessment and Health Surveillance Questionnaire.

Any concern about animal care or welfare should be reported directly; see the IACUC policy on concern reporting.

Motion-capture cameras 📸 — read the Optitrack methodology document and work through the Flex 13 system with a graduate student before running experiments.

Avian specimen handling — sign the Avian Specimen Handling SOP and complete an introductory walkthrough with a graduate student.

Lab inspections

Monthly: emergency-eyewash test and fire-extinguisher visual checks. Every six months: a lab cleaning day with the inspection checklist and self-inspection. Annually: an internal EH&S General and Occupational Safety inspection.

Documentation

The Injury and Illness Prevention Plan (IIPP) and Emergency Action Plan (EAP) are maintained through the MAE Department (Safety). Report injuries via UC Davis injury reporting.
